title: Exotic Spin Excitation Continuum in a Weakly Coupled Quantum Chainsaw Antiferromagnet
authors: Asiri Thennakoon, Prena Chaudhary, Sankha Subhra Bakshi, Tommy Park, Tristen Lowrey, Daniel Pajerowski, Christina Hoffmann, Junghong H. He, Hiroaki Ueda, Collin Broholm, Gia-Wei Chern, Seung-Hun Lee
abstract: Collective motions in strongly interacting magnets involve many spins and are often described in terms of integer-spin excitations. However, in certain cases, the collective motion can behave as if these integer excitations break apart into smaller, particle-like entities with unusual properties. Such fractionalized excitations in quantum magnets are commonly associated either with topological order in two dimensions or with criticality in one dimension. It remains unclear how these distinct mechanisms are connected across a dimensional crossover. Here we investigate the Ti-based quantum antiferromagnet, $Cs_{8}LiNa_{3}Ti_{12}F_{48}$, in which $Ti^{3+}$ ($3d^{1}$, $S=1/2$) ions interact antiferromagnetically within distorted kagome planes. Our inelastic neutron scattering study on a single crystal reveals a frustrated network of weakly coupled spin-$1/2$ chainsaws, realizing a regime of dimensional frustration in which interchain couplings fail to establish coherent two-dimensional order. The magnetic excitation spectrum exhibits a strong continuum spanning the full measured momentum and energy phase space. In addition, the dynamic spin correlation function displays rod-like scattering in momentum space, indicating a quasi-one-dimensional nature of the magnetic correlations. These results point to fractionalized excitations with intrinsically directional character, demonstrating that signatures of one-dimensional criticality can persist within a two-dimensional lattice. Our findings establish anisotropic fractionalization as a distinct organizing principle for quantum-disordered states.
summary: This paper identifies a unique state of 'anisotropic fractionalization' in a Ti-based kagome-related antiferromagnet. By using neutron scattering, the authors show that a 2D lattice can behave as a network of weakly coupled 1D chains, demonstrating that 1D-like quantum criticality can survive within a 2D framework.
main_problem: The study investigates the dimensional crossover of quantum disorder, specifically how fractionalized excitations evolve when transitioning from 1D criticality to 2D topological order in a frustrated lattice.
main_result: The researchers discovered a regime of 'anisotropic fractionalization' in a Ti-based kagome-related magnet, where 2D lattice dynamics reorganize into a network of weakly coupled spin-1/2 chains, maintaining 1D-like signatures.
method: The study combines single-crystal neutron diffraction for structural refinement with inelastic neutron scattering (INS) using the CNCS and TOPAZ spectrometers at the Spallation Neutron Source.
model_or_system: The physical system is the Ti-based quantum antiferromagnet Cs8LiNa3Ti12F48, which features a distorted kagome lattice of Ti3+ (S=1/2) ions and an anisotropic exchange Hamiltonian with four distinct nearest-neighbor couplings and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions.
key_observables: Dynamic spin correlation function S(Q, h-omega), equal-time response function S(Q), magnetic susceptibility, and crystal structure refinement statistics.
important_parameters: Exchange couplings (JA=11.26, JB=3.20, JC=8.15, JD=1.22 meV), Curie-Weiss temperature (theta_CW = -36.9 K), and measurement temperatures (1.7 K and 30 K).
main_assumptions: The structural refinement assumed a fixed position for the Lithium atom due to high correlation with the local background; the magnetic model assumes a 2D nearest-neighbor kagome framework.
figures_summary: The figures likely include crystal structure refinements, magnetic susceptibility plots, and momentum-resolved inelastic neutron scattering maps showing the excitation continuum and rod-like scattering.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dimensional crossover, describes the crystal structure and synthesis of the Ti-based magnet, presents neutron diffraction and susceptibility data, details the inelastic neutron scattering results showing the excitation continuum, and concludes with a discussion on anisotropic fractionalization.
why_interesting: The paper explores how many-body dynamics and fractionalization can persist across a dimensional crossover, which is highly relevant to understanding the stability of exotic excitations in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 | Neutron diffraction study of Cs8LiNa3Ti12F48. (a) Comparison of the observed (𝐹𝑜𝑏𝑠) and calculated (𝐹𝑐𝑎𝑙𝑐) structure factors, derived from time-of-flight neutron data and crystal structure refinement using the Jana2020 package, respectively, demonstrating good agreement between experiment and the refined model. (b) Polyhedral representation of the complete three- dimensional monoclinic structure (space group Cm). The nonmagnetic alkali-metal cations (Cs, Na, Li) occupy the structural voids, acting as physical spacers between the magnetic Ti–F networks. Figure 2 | Temperature dependence of the bulk magnetic susceptibility of Cs8LiNa3Ti12F48, Magnetic susceptibility, 𝜒= 𝑀/𝐻, measured on a single crystal in an applied field of 𝜇0𝐻= 1 T with the field perpendicular to the kagome plane (blue circles) and parallel to the kagome plane (red circles). The two curves nearly overlap over the full measured temperature range, indicating weak bulk magnetic anisotropy. The inset on the top right shows the inverse susceptibility 1/𝜒 together with a Curie-Weiss fit to the high-temperature region (dashed blue line), yielding a Curie- Weiss temperature Θ𝐶𝑊= −36.9 K and 𝑝𝑒𝑓𝑓= 1.693 𝜇𝐵.

Figure 4 | Dynamic spin correlations in Cs8LiNa3Ti12F48. Color contour maps of the normalized dynamic spin correlation function, 𝑆(𝐐, ℏ𝜔), as a function of momentum (𝐐) and energy transfer (ℏ𝜔), measured along two representative momentum cuts through the two-dimensional Brillouin zone, indicated by the green arrows in the insets. For each cut, the top panel shows the data at 1.7 K, the middle panel shows the data at 30 K, and the bottom panel shows the corresponding linear spin-wave theory (LSWT) intensity, 𝑆𝐿𝑆𝑊𝑇(𝐐,ℏ𝜔), calculated for the anisotropic J-D model.
